entries.title: Lagrange Multipliers
entries.content: A strategy for finding the local maxima and minima of a function subject to equality constraints.
entries.example: Maximizing utility subject to a budget constraint

entries.title: Lagrange's Theorem Groups
entries.content: Theorem stating that for any finite group G, the order of every subgroup H divides the order of G.
entries.example: A group of order 6 can only have subgroups of order 1, 2, 3, or 6

entries.title: Laplace Transform
entries.content: An integral transform that converts a function of a real variable (often time) to a function of a complex variable.
entries.formula: L{f(t)} = integral from 0 to infinity of e^(-s*t)*f(t) dt
entries.example: Solving linear differential equations in control theory

entries.title: Laplace's Equation
entries.content: A second-order partial differential equation whose solutions are harmonic functions.
entries.example: Finding steady-state temperature distribution on a plate

entries.title: Law of Cosines
entries.content: Generalization of the Pythagorean theorem relating the lengths of sides of a triangle to the cosine of one of its angles.
entries.example: Finding the third side of a triangle when two sides and the included angle are known

entries.title: Law of Large Numbers
entries.content: Theorem describing the result of performing the same experiment a large number of times, where average approaches expected value.
entries.formula: limit as n->inf of X_bar = mu
entries.example: The percentage of heads in coin flips approaches 50% as flips increase

entries.title: Law of Sines
entries.content: Equation relating the lengths of the sides of any shape triangle to the sines of its angles.
entries.formula: a / sin(A) = b / sin(B) = c / sin(C)
entries.example: Solving a triangle given two angles and one side

entries.title: Law of Syllogism
entries.content: A rule of inference in propositional logic stating that if p implies q and q implies r, then p implies r.
entries.example: If it rains, streets get wet; if streets get wet, cars slip; therefore if it rains, cars slip

entries.title: Least Common Multiple
entries.content: The smallest positive integer that is divisible by two or more numbers.

entries.title: Least Squares Regression
entries.content: A method for finding the best-fitting curve to a given set of points by minimizing the sum of the squares of the residuals.
entries.example: Fitting a trendline to scatterplot data points

entries.title: Lebesgue Integral
entries.content: An alternative definition of the integral based on measure theory, generalising the Riemann integral.
entries.example: Integrating highly discontinuous functions like the indicator function of rationals

entries.title: Legendre Polynomials
entries.content: A system of complete and orthogonal polynomials that solve Legendre's differential equation.
entries.example: Solving potential theory problems in spherical coordinates

entries.title: Leibniz Rule
entries.content: Formula for the derivative of the product of two functions, or for differentiating under the integral sign.
entries.example: Differentiating x * sin(x) to get sin(x) + x*cos(x)

entries.title: Lemma in Mathematics
entries.content: A helper proposition or minor theorem used as a stepping stone to prove a larger theorem.
entries.example: Euclid's lemma used to prove unique factorization

entries.title: Length of Vector
entries.content: The magnitude of a vector, calculated as the square root of the sum of its squared components.
entries.example: Length of vector [3, 4] is 5

entries.title: L'Hopital's Rule
entries.content: A method in calculus for evaluating limits of indeterminate forms using derivatives.
entries.example: Evaluating limit of sin(x)/x as x approaches 0

entries.title: Lie Algebra
entries.content: A non-associative algebra whose multiplication is anticommutative and satisfies the Jacobi identity.
entries.example: Matrix commutator algebra [A, B] = AB - BA

entries.title: Lie Group
entries.content: A group that is also a differentiable manifold, with smooth group operations.
entries.formula: G is manifold, product is smooth
entries.example: The rotation group SO(3) representing 3D rotations

entries.title: Limit Definition
entries.content: The value that a function approaches as the input approaches some value.
entries.example: Limit of 1/x as x goes to infinity is 0

entries.title: Limit Laws
entries.content: Rules for evaluating limits of combinations of functions algebraically.
entries.formula: lim (f(x) + g(x)) = lim f(x) + lim g(x)
entries.example: Finding limit of x^2 + 3x as x approaches 2

entries.title: Line Equation Slope-Intercept
entries.content: Equation of a straight line using its slope and y-intercept.
entries.example: Line with slope 2 passing through y-axis at (0, 3) is y = 2x + 3

entries.title: Linear Combination
entries.content: An expression constructed from a set of terms by multiplying each term by a constant and adding the results.
entries.example: Vector [5, 8] written as 5*[1,0] + 8*[0,1]

entries.title: Linear Dependence
entries.content: A set of vectors where at least one vector can be written as a linear combination of the others.
entries.example: Vectors [1, 2] and [2, 4] are linearly dependent

entries.title: Linear Equation
entries.content: An algebraic equation of the first degree, whose graph is a straight line.

entries.title: Linear Independence
entries.content: A set of vectors where no vector can be written as a linear combination of the others.
entries.example: Vectors [1, 0] and [0, 1] are linearly independent

entries.title: Linear Programming
entries.content: Method to achieve the best outcome in a mathematical model whose requirements are represented by linear relationships.
entries.formula: max c^T * x subject to A*x <= b
entries.example: Optimizing factory output subject to resource constraints

entries.title: Linear Regression
entries.content: Linear approach to modeling the relationship between a scalar response and one or more explanatory variables.
entries.example: Modeling weight based on height metrics

entries.title: Linear Transformation
entries.content: A mapping between two vector spaces that preserves vector addition and scalar multiplication.
entries.formula: T(u + v) = T(u) + T(v); T(c*u) = c*T(u)
entries.example: Rotating coordinates 90 degrees around origin

entries.title: Linearity of Expectation
entries.content: Property stating that the expectation of a sum of random variables is the sum of their expectations.
entries.example: Expected sum of rolling two dice is expected of first plus second

entries.title: Lipschitz Continuity
entries.content: A strong form of uniform continuity where the slope of the secant line never exceeds a constant.
entries.example: f(x) = sin(x) is Lipschitz continuous with L = 1

entries.title: Local Maximum
entries.content: A point where a function's value is greater than or equal to its values at nearby points.
entries.formula: f(c) >= f(x) for all x near c
entries.example: The peak of y = 4 - x^2 at (0, 4)

entries.title: Local Minimum
entries.content: A point where a function's value is less than or equal to its values at nearby points.
entries.formula: f(c) <= f(x) for all x near c
entries.example: The trough of y = x^2 - 3 at (0, -3)

entries.title: Logarithm Base Conversion
entries.content: Converting a logarithm of one base to another base using quotients.
entries.example: Converting log base 2 to natural log

entries.title: Logarithm Change of Base
entries.content: The change of base formula for logarithms.
entries.example: Evaluating log2(5) as ln(5)/ln(2)

entries.title: Logarithm Definition Natural
entries.content: A logarithm with base e, the mathematical constant approximately 2.718.

entries.title: Logarithm Product Rule
entries.content: Property stating the logarithm of a product is the sum of the logarithms.
entries.example: log(20) = log(2) + log(10)

entries.title: Logarithm Quotient Rule
entries.content: Property stating the logarithm of a quotient is the difference of the logarithms.
entries.example: log(5) = log(10) - log(2)

entries.title: Logarithmic Differentiation
entries.content: A method of differentiating functions by taking the natural logarithm first.
entries.example: Differentiating y = x^x to get y' = x^x * (ln(x) + 1)

entries.title: Logistic Growth Function
entries.content: A model for population growth that slows as it approaches a carrying capacity.
entries.example: Modeling population growth in a limited environment

entries.title: Lower Triangular Matrix
entries.content: A square matrix in which all entries above the main diagonal are zero.
entries.example: Matrix with zeroes on top right section
